final_subgenre: Single-craft psychological thriller — thriller-primary, standalone-first, KU/digital-optimized (McFadden ceiling model). NOT a two-genre romance-thriller crossover, and NOT espionage.
refined_positioning: The "obsession thriller" niche: dark, morally-grey, desire-charged domestic/psychological suspense in the You (Kepnes) / Verity (Hoover) lane — an antihero or unreliable obsessive at the center, a toxic-intimacy engine, no guaranteed HEA. Built and shelved as ONE craft (thriller: unreliable narration, escalating dread, twist payoff); the romantic/obsessive charge is tonal seasoning delivered through thriller tools, not a co-equal romance arc. Concrete build spec: single primary Amazon category (Psychological Thrillers / Domestic Thrillers, not Romance), standalone-first for twist-driven word-of-mouth, author-brand backlit rather than serialized-couple read-through, rapid-release cadence, BookTok-native "he/she is not who they seem + I can't stop wanting them" hooks. This keeps velocity high (one engine to master per book) while feeding — not splitting — the also-bought graph.
how_skeptics_addressed: 1) DUAL-CRAFT / BUILDABILITY TAX (all three skeptics' #1 risk): eliminated. There is one plot engine (thriller). No mandatory romance beat-mastery, no HEA obligation, no ~60/40 intertwined arcs — the obsession is rendered with suspense craft the author already owns, so per-book velocity (the mechanic KU rewards) is protected. 2) READER MIGRATION to dark/villain/antihero (skeptic 1) and dark-romance surge (skeptics 2 & 3): embraced rather than fought — the obsession thriller IS the antihero/dark lane, aimed at where 2026 desire-energy is going, not the departing "light danger + HEA" lane. We capture dark-romance READER SPILLOVER through a thriller frame instead of paying romance-craft costs. 3) THRILLER PRINT DECLINE (skeptic 1's -2.2% print stat): irrelevant to the chosen channel — the proof point (McFadden) is a KU/digital indie ceiling in exactly this psychological-thriller niche, which is up, not the shrinking print aisle. 4) SPLIT-CHART DISCOVERABILITY TRAP (all three): resolved by a single primary category and coherent also-bought graph; the book feeds tight niching (the spy-thriller case study that gained by narrowing) instead of straddling two tribes and eating "not what I expected" 1-stars. 5) READ-THROUGH FRAGILITY (skeptics 2 & 3): standalone-first + twist word-of-mouth removes the series-completion and cross-tribe-leakage tax; backlist compounds on author brand, not on re-committing to one couple/world. 6) INCUMBENT WALL (skeptics 2 & 3): the indie psychological-thriller ceiling is demonstrably breakable (McFadden broke through recently as an indie), unlike the romantic-suspense charts locked by Roberts/Brown/Garwood; obsession/desire hooks also unlock the free BookTok organic channel a no-ad-budget solo needs. 7) ESPIONAGE runner-up: correctly dropped, as all three skeptics advise — narrower, research-heavy, male-skewing, series-dependent, a harder solo sell. 8) FULL DARK-ROMANCE PIVOT (skeptics' alt (2)): rejected because it abandons the author's thriller craft identity and reintroduces romance-arc mastery + HEA obligation; we harvest that reader demand through the thriller instead.
white_space: The gap between two crowded-but-distinct lanes: (a) clinical, emotionally-cold twist-thrillers (McFadden-style domestic suspense) that convert on plot but give BookTok readers no desire/relationship charge to swoon and evangelize over; and (b) dark romance / dark romantasy, which delivers that charge but demands a guaranteed HEA, romance-craft, and shelves outside thriller. The obsession thriller sits precisely between them: thriller-shelved and single-craft (so it's buildable solo and niches tightly for the algorithm), yet carrying the toxic-intimacy, morally-grey, "I shouldn't want them" emotional voltage that is pulling romance/BookTok readers in 2026. It converts the dark-romance reader migration and the antihero appetite into thriller sales WITHOUT the HEA promise or the dual-craft tax — capturing spillover demand that pure psychological thrillers leave on the table and that dark romance can't serve to readers who want a real twist ending over a guaranteed happy one.
